serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve bowl of Spicy Diced Chicken Rice Noodle weighs approximately 400 g, comprising rice noodles, diced chicken pieces, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Diced Chicken Rice Noodle 云南辣子鸡小锅米线
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010494) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record. Saturated fat is estimated at ~1.0 g/100g based on the fat profile of lean chicken and chili oil typical of this dish, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this field is therefore marked as adapted.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI analogous estimate based on rice noodle soup entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Yunnan Spicy Diced Chicken Rice Noodle. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. The protein (4.96 g/100g) and fat (4.0 g/100g) content from chicken and chili oil will modestly attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load calculated as: GI (61) × carbohydrate per serving (60.5 g for 400 g serving) / 100 = 36.9, rounded to 37.1 accounting for mixed-dish attenuation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100g with insufficient potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: At 324 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,296 mg per 400 g serving), this dish provides over half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced seasoning, less broth, and verify that chili-based sauces do not add further sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI rice noodles with moderate carbohydrate load; low dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice noodles carry a medium GI (~61) and the dish delivers approximately 60 g of carbohydrate per standard 400 g serving, producing a glycaemic load of ~37; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request additional non-starchy vegetables to raise fibre content, and pair with a protein-rich side to further blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat; low saturated fat is favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is relatively low in saturated fat (~1.0 g/100g) and cholesterol (13 mg/100g), which is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content (324 mg/100g) poses a risk for blood pressure elevation and endothelial stress; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size and request a reduced-sodium preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredient (dried chili); high sodium; low anti-inflammatory nutrient density
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried chili is a nightshade ingredient that may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive RA patients; combined with high sodium (324 mg/100g) that can worsen tissue swelling, individuals with RA should monitor tolerance, request less broth and seasoning, and supplement with anti-inflammatory vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Diced Chicken Rice Noodle (云南辣子鸡小锅米线, Yúnnán Làzi Jī Xiǎoguō Mǐxiàn) is a Yunnan-style Chinese noodle soup dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and casual dining restaurants. The dish features smooth, silky rice noodles (米线, mǐxiàn) submerged in a fragrant, spicy broth made with dried chili, Sichuan peppercorn, garlic, ginger, and soy-based seasoning, topped with tender diced chicken pieces and garnished with spring onion and chili oil. The flavour profile is bold, numbing-spicy, and savoury, with a characteristic tingly heat from Sichuan peppercorn. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate, with 1.0 g dietary fibre and 324 mg sodium. The medium glycaemic index (~61) from rice noodles means carbohydrate is absorbed at a moderate rate, but the low fibre content limits glycaemic modulation. Clinically, the dish is borderline for hypertension due to its sodium content, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium-GI carbohydrate load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to sodium despite low saturated fat. It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Chinese food landscape, representing the Yunnan culinary tradition of small-pot rice noodle soups.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010494) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The dish is an exact match in the HPB canonical database. Saturated fat is the only adapted field, estimated from the known fat composition of lean chicken and chili oil. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.0 g/100g) is an adapted estimate based on the typical fat profile of lean diced chicken (~0.6 g sat fat/100g chicken) and chili oil contribution; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Yunnan rice noodle dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 4. Halal status is marked true as the dish contains chicken and plant-based ingredients with no pork or alcohol; however, halal certification should be verified with the specific vendor. 5. The dish name 云南辣子鸡小锅米线 refers specifically to the Yunnan small-pot preparation style (小锅, xiǎoguō), distinguishing it from other rice noodle formats.
